flashcards.front: What are the fundamental/primitive data types in C++?
flashcards.back: C++ has 5 fundamental data types: int (integers), float (floating-point), double (double precision), char (characters), and bool (true/false). void represents absence of type.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between int, short, and long?
flashcards.back: short is at least 16 bits, int is at least 16 bits (usually 32), long is at least 32 bits, and long long is at least 64 bits. They differ in storage size and range.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between signed and unsigned modifiers?
flashcards.back: signed allows negative and positive values (uses 2's complement). unsigned allows only non-negative values (0 and positive), effectively doubling the maximum positive range.

flashcards.front: What is type conversion in C++?
flashcards.back: Type conversion is converting one data type to another. Implicit conversion happens automatically by the compiler. Explicit conversion uses cast operators like (type), static_cast, or type().

flashcards.front: What is the difference between = and == operators?
flashcards.back: = is the assignment operator that assigns a value to a variable. == is the equality/relational operator that compares two values and returns true or false.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ++i and i++?
flashcards.back: ++i (pre-increment) increments i then returns the new value. i++ (post-increment) returns the current value then increments i. Pre-increment is faster in expressions as it doesn't need a temporary copy.

flashcards.front: What are logical operators in C++?
flashcards.back: && (logical AND - true if both operands true), || (logical OR - true if either operand true), ! (logical NOT - inverts boolean value).

flashcards.front: What are bitwise operators in C++?
flashcards.back: & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT/ones complement), << (left shift), >> (right shift). They operate at bit level on integers.

flashcards.front: What is the ternary operator in C++?
flashcards.back: The ternary operator (? :) is a conditional operator: condition ? expression1 : expression2. Returns expression1 if true, expression2 if false.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between const and constexpr?
flashcards.back: const is for variables that won't change after initialization (runtime constant). constexpr is for compile-time constants - values that can be calculated during compilation.

flashcards.front: What are variable storage classes in C++?
flashcards.back: auto (automatic/local), register (request fast access), static (lifetime throughout program), extern (external linkage). They define scope, lifetime, and storage location.
sizeof returns the size in bytes of a data type or variable. Syntax: sizeof(type) or sizeof(variable). Useful for determining platform-specific sizes.

flashcards.front: What are relational operators in C++?
flashcards.back: == (equal to), != (not equal to), > (greater than), < (less than), >= (greater than or equal), <= (less than or equal). All return boolean values.
Type casting explicitly converts one type to another. C-style: (int)x. C++ style: static_cast<int>(x), const_cast, reinterpret_cast, dynamic_cast.
The comma operator evaluates expressions from left to right and returns the value of the rightmost expression. Used in for loops: for(i=0,j=0; ; i++,j++).
